lawTitle: Never Outshine the Master
scenarioText: During the client pitch, a new analyst jumps in to correct her director's numbers in front of everyone, then adds three ideas he hadn't thought of. The room is impressed with her. A week later she's quietly moved off the account and given grunt work. Her insights were right, but her timing made her boss look small.
explanationText: This is Never Outshine the Master. When you make a superior feel outdone in public, you trigger their insecurity rather than their gratitude, and they use their power to push you back down. Displaying more brilliance than the person above you is often punished, not rewarded.

lawTitle: Never Put Too Much Trust in Friends, Learn How to Use Enemies
scenarioText: Two best friends open a cafe together, sure that closeness guarantees loyalty. Within a year the friend feels entitled, slacks off, and resents taking direction. Meanwhile a former rival, hired only for his skill, works twice as hard to prove himself. The friendship curdles into the ugliest breakup of the business.
explanationText: This illustrates Never Put Too Much Trust in Friends, Learn How to Use Enemies. Friends expect favor and grow complacent, and betrayal from them cuts deepest. A former enemy, eager to earn trust, often becomes the more reliable and motivated ally.

lawTitle: Conceal Your Intentions
scenarioText: A homebuyer tours a house she desperately wants. Instead of gushing, she frowns at the kitchen, mentions two other listings she's seeing, and asks casually about how long it's been on the market. The sellers, sensing a lukewarm buyer, drop the price before she even makes an offer.
explanationText: This is Conceal Your Intentions. By hiding how much she wanted the house and pointing at false alternatives, she kept the other side guessing and off-balance. When people can't read your true aim, they can't defend against it or use it as leverage.

lawTitle: Always Say Less Than Necessary
scenarioText: In a salary negotiation the recruiter names a number, then goes silent. The candidate, uneasy in the pause, fills it by explaining why he'd accept less and rambling about his weaknesses. He talks himself down five thousand dollars. The recruiter simply had to wait.
explanationText: This demonstrates Always Say Less Than Necessary. The more you say, the more ordinary and exposed you appear, and the more ammunition you hand the other side. Silence unnerves people and makes them reveal themselves; restraint keeps power on your side of the table.

lawTitle: So Much Depends on Reputation — Guard It with Your Life
scenarioText: A freelance designer has spent years being known as the person who never misses a deadline. A jealous ex-collaborator starts hinting online that she ghosts clients. Instead of ignoring it, she immediately posts testimonials and a public project tracker, snuffing out the rumor before it spreads. Her whole livelihood rests on that one word: reliable.
explanationText: This is So Much Depends on Reputation — Guard It with Your Life. Reputation is the shield that lets people trust you sight unseen, and a single unanswered attack can unravel it. She defended it fiercely because one crack in that image threatens everything built on it.

lawTitle: Court Attention at All Costs
scenarioText: Two chefs open restaurants on the same street. One quietly serves excellent food. The other stages loud pop-ups, feuds publicly with a food critic, and films outrageous kitchen stunts. Six months later the second chef is fully booked and the first, equally talented, sits half empty.
explanationText: This is Court Attention at All Costs. Being good is not enough if no one is looking; visibility itself becomes power. By making himself impossible to ignore, even through controversy, the loud chef drew the crowd, because attention is a currency the quiet often never collect.

lawTitle: Get Others to Do the Work for You, but Always Take the Credit
scenarioText: In a college group project, one student lets the others research and build the slides, contributing almost nothing. When it's time to present, he volunteers to be the speaker. He delivers their work smoothly, fields the professor's questions, and walks away remembered as the one who carried the team.
explanationText: This is Get Others to Do the Work for You, but Always Take the Credit. The labor is invisible; the presentation is what people remember and reward. By positioning himself at the point of visibility, he harvested recognition for effort that wasn't his.

lawTitle: Make Other People Come to You — Use Bait if Necessary
scenarioText: A woman keeps matching with someone on a dating app who suggests they meet at his favorite bar across town, on his schedule. Instead she says she's busy this week but he's welcome to join her friend's rooftop thing Friday. He rearranges his plans and shows up early, already on her turf.
explanationText: This is Make Other People Come to You — Use Bait if Necessary. By refusing to chase and dangling an attractive invitation on her own ground, she forced him to move toward her. When the other person comes to you, they arrive already committing energy, and you control the terms of the encounter.

lawTitle: Win Through Your Actions, Never Through Argument
scenarioText: A teenager's parents insist his side hustle is a waste of time, and dinners turn into shouting matches where no one budges. He stops arguing entirely. Months later he quietly pays for his own car and shows the deposit slips from his customers. The debate ends without another word.
explanationText: This is Win Through Your Actions, Never Through Argument. Words breed resentment and rarely change a made-up mind, even when you're right. By letting results speak, he produced proof no counter-argument could touch and won lasting agreement instead of a temporary point.

lawTitle: Infection: Avoid the Unhappy and Unlucky
scenarioText: One friend in the group is perpetually in crisis: dramatic breakups, feuds, money he never repays. Those closest to him find themselves anxious, broke, and dragged into his conflicts. A newer friend notices the pattern early and gently keeps her distance, and her own life stays remarkably calm.
explanationText: This is Infection: Avoid the Unhappy and Unlucky. Certain people spread their chaos and misfortune to everyone who gets close, draining time, money, and peace. Recognizing the pattern and stepping back is self-protection, because emotional states are contagious.

lawTitle: Learn to Keep People Dependent on You
scenarioText: A freelance developer builds a company's entire website but deliberately never documents how it works or shares clean access. Every time they want a small change, they must call him, and he alone can fix it. They've talked about replacing him for years, yet they can't afford to.
explanationText: This is Learn to Keep People Dependent on You. By making himself the single point of knowledge, he becomes irreplaceable, and their need locks him in place. Security comes not from being liked but from being the one others cannot do without.

lawTitle: Use Selective Honesty and Generosity to Disarm Your Victim
scenarioText: A car salesman opens by pointing out a real flaw in the model the customer likes and even talks him out of an expensive add-on. Grateful for the candor, the customer drops his guard completely and trusts every later claim, signing on a pricier trim without a second look.
explanationText: This is Use Selective Honesty and Generosity to Disarm Your Victim. One genuine, well-timed act of honesty buys credibility that covers everything after it. A single sincere gesture lowers suspicion far more effectively than a hundred smooth pitches.

lawTitle: When Asking for Help, Appeal to People's Self-Interest, Never to Their Mercy or Gratitude
scenarioText: A founder needs a busy former mentor to introduce him to investors. He doesn't remind her of past favors or beg. Instead he shows how the intro positions her as an early backer of a hot deal and offers her advisory equity. She makes the call within the hour.
explanationText: This is When Asking for Help, Appeal to People's Self-Interest, Never to Their Mercy or Gratitude. Reminding people of debts breeds resentment, but showing them what they gain makes helping feel like winning. Frame the request around their advantage and they move eagerly.

lawTitle: Pose as a Friend, Work as a Spy
scenarioText: At an industry mixer, a rep from a competing firm is warm and curious, buying rounds and asking friendly questions about roadmaps and pricing struggles. A junior manager, flattered by the attention, spills more than he should. Weeks later that competitor undercuts every deal with uncanny precision.
explanationText: This is Pose as a Friend, Work as a Spy. Under the cover of friendly interest, one side gathered intelligence the other volunteered freely. Warmth and casual questions disarm people into revealing exactly the information you need to outmaneuver them.

lawTitle: Crush Your Enemy Totally
scenarioText: A startup wins a lawsuit against a copycat rival but, feeling merciful, settles for a small payment and lets them keep operating. The rival regroups, raises money on the publicity, and returns two years later to steal half the market. The founder wishes he'd finished it when he had the chance.
explanationText: This is Crush Your Enemy Totally. A defeated adversary left with resources and resentment will recover and seek revenge. Half-measures create future threats; ending a rivalry decisively is safer than leaving embers that can reignite into a fire.

lawTitle: Use Absence to Increase Respect and Honor
scenarioText: A guy texts a new match constantly, double-messaging within minutes and always available for last-minute plans. Her interest fades. His friend, dating someone new, replies warmly but takes his time and stays busy with his own life. She finds herself thinking about him far more.
explanationText: This is Use Absence to Increase Respect and Honor. Constant presence makes you common and easy to take for granted, while measured scarcity makes you valued and pursued. Stepping back at the right moment increases your worth in others' eyes.

lawTitle: Keep Others in Suspended Terror: Cultivate an Air of Unpredictability
scenarioText: A basketball coach is impossible to read: some games he benches stars for one lazy possession, other times he lets mistakes slide. Players never quite know where they stand, so they hustle on every play to stay safe. His locker room is tense but relentlessly sharp.
explanationText: This is Keep Others in Suspended Terror: Cultivate an Air of Unpredictability. When people can't anticipate your reactions, they stay alert and off-balance, working harder to avoid your displeasure. Predictable leaders get managed; unpredictable ones keep everyone on their toes.

lawTitle: Do Not Build Fortresses to Protect Yourself — Isolation Is Dangerous
scenarioText: Burned by a leak, a startup CEO stops attending industry events, cuts off gossipy peers, and communicates only through a tiny inner circle. Cut off from the network, he misses a major shift in the market and a hiring wave that guts his team before he even hears it's coming.
explanationText: This is Do Not Build Fortresses to Protect Yourself — Isolation Is Dangerous. Retreating from the world may feel safe, but it cuts you off from information, allies, and early warnings. Power depends on circulation among people; isolation leaves you blind and exposed.

lawTitle: Know Who You're Dealing With — Do Not Offend the Wrong Person
scenarioText: A contractor lowballs and mocks a quiet older client over a small invoice dispute, assuming she's a pushover. She turns out to be a well-connected board member who quietly warns every developer in the county. His phone stops ringing, and he never learns exactly why.
explanationText: This is Know Who You're Dealing With — Do Not Offend the Wrong Person. People are not interchangeable, and the mild-seeming can wield hidden influence. Failing to read someone's true reach before crossing them can invite retaliation far beyond the original slight.

lawTitle: Do Not Commit to Anyone
scenarioText: Two rival factions form at a startup, each pressuring a talented engineer to pick a side. She stays cordial to both, commits to neither, and keeps her options open. When the dust settles and one side wins, she's the only person both camps still trust, and she's promoted.
explanationText: This is Do Not Commit to Anyone. By refusing to be captured by either faction, she stayed independent and let others expend themselves in the fight. Holding back from full commitment keeps you courted by all sides and beholden to none.

lawTitle: Play a Sucker to Catch a Sucker — Seem Dumber Than Your Mark
scenarioText: In a used-car haggle, the buyer plays a bumbling first-timer, fumbling with terms and asking naive questions. The seller, sure he's got an easy mark, relaxes and reveals his real bottom line. The 'clueless' buyer promptly names that exact number and walks away with a steal.
explanationText: This is Play a Sucker to Catch a Sucker — Seem Dumber Than Your Mark. Letting someone feel smarter than you makes them careless and eager to show off. By hiding his sharpness, the buyer lured the seller into exposing information he'd never have shared with an equal.

lawTitle: Use the Surrender Tactic: Transform Weakness into Power
scenarioText: A junior employee is publicly blamed for a failed launch by a powerful VP. Instead of fighting, she calmly accepts responsibility and offers to lead the fix. Her graciousness makes the VP look like a bully, and colleagues rally to her. Within a quarter she has his old team.
explanationText: This is Use the Surrender Tactic: Transform Weakness into Power. When you can't win a direct fight, yielding denies your opponent the satisfaction of the kill and buys you time and sympathy. Apparent surrender can quietly reverse who holds the moral high ground.

lawTitle: Concentrate Your Forces
scenarioText: A musician spreads herself thin across five platforms, three side gigs, and a half-finished album, and none of it takes off. Exhausted, she cancels everything but one weekly show and pours all her energy there. Within a year that single focused venue makes her a local name.
explanationText: This is Concentrate Your Forces. Scattering effort across many fronts dilutes your impact until nothing lands. By channeling everything into one rich, focused source, she achieved the intensity that thin, divided effort never could.

lawTitle: Play the Perfect Courtier
scenarioText: In a political office full of egos, one aide never openly flatters yet always makes his boss look brilliant, defers gracefully in meetings, and disagrees only in private and softly. He never seems to be maneuvering. Quietly, he becomes the most trusted person in the building.
explanationText: This is Play the Perfect Courtier. Thriving in a world of power means mastering indirection: appearing agreeable, making superiors shine, and never letting your ambition show. His smooth, unthreatening manner is exactly what lets him rise.

lawTitle: Re-Create Yourself
scenarioText: After being typecast as the shy 'tech guy,' a man deliberately reinvents himself: new wardrobe, a bold speaking style, a distinctive personal brand online, even a new way of introducing himself. People who meet him now see a confident leader, and old labels no longer stick.
explanationText: This is Re-Create Yourself. Rather than accepting the identity others assigned him, he consciously authored a new public persona. Taking control of how you're perceived, instead of letting circumstances define you, is a powerful act of self-invention.

lawTitle: Keep Your Hands Clean
scenarioText: A manager wants to fire an unpopular team member but doesn't want the blame. He nudges a trusted lieutenant to raise the complaints and 'make the call,' then expresses public regret about the outcome. The team's resentment lands on the lieutenant while the manager stays beloved.
explanationText: This is Keep Your Hands Clean. He got his desired result while ensuring the ugliness attached to someone else. Using a cat's-paw to do the dirty work lets you enjoy the benefits of an action while keeping your own reputation spotless.

lawTitle: Play on People's Need to Believe to Create a Cultlike Following
scenarioText: A wellness coach sells not just workouts but a whole identity: vague promises of transformation, insider language, group rituals, and enemies to define the tribe. Followers defend her fiercely and recruit friends. The vaguer the promise, the more devoted the community becomes.
explanationText: This is Play on People's Need to Believe to Create a Cultlike Following. By offering belonging, ritual, and a grand cause instead of concrete proof, she satisfies a deep hunger for meaning. People crave something to believe in, and a movement built on that need becomes self-sustaining.

lawTitle: Enter Action with Boldness
scenarioText: Asked to present to executives at the last minute, a nervous analyst almost declines, then decides to own the room. He walks in decisive, states his recommendation flatly, and never hedges. The leaders, reading confidence as competence, approve his plan on the spot.
explanationText: This is Enter Action with Boldness. Hesitation signals doubt and invites others to doubt you too, while boldness projects competence and sweeps people along. Committing fully to a course, even an uncertain one, commands more respect than timid half-measures.

lawTitle: Plan All the Way to the End
scenarioText: A couple launches a wedding-planning business focused only on booking clients, thrilled when orders pour in. They never mapped out staffing, cash flow, or peak-season overlap. By summer they're double-booked, drowning, and refunding furious customers, undone by success they didn't prepare for.
explanationText: This is Plan All the Way to the End. They fixated on the opening move and ignored the consequences that followed. Anticipating every stage to the conclusion, including complications, is what separates lasting victories from ones that collapse under their own momentum.

lawTitle: Make Your Accomplishments Seem Effortless
scenarioText: A creator posts a flawless video that clearly hit millions and makes it look casual, as if filmed on a whim. She never mentions the forty takes, the reshoots, or the two days of editing. Audiences marvel at her natural talent, and the hidden labor only deepens the awe.
explanationText: This is Make Your Accomplishments Seem Effortless. By concealing the sweat behind her work, she makes her skill look like magic rather than grind. When you hide the effort, people credit you with a rare gift, and visible struggle would only shrink their admiration.

lawTitle: Control the Options: Get Others to Play with the Cards You Deal
scenarioText: A parent doesn't ask her toddler whether he'll eat his vegetables. She asks if he wants the broccoli or the peas first. He picks, feeling powerful and independent, and eats both. Either choice leads exactly where she intended, and the fight never happens.
explanationText: This is Control the Options: Get Others to Play with the Cards You Deal. By narrowing the choices to ones that all serve her, she gives the illusion of freedom while steering the outcome. People resist being forced but embrace choosing, even when every option is yours.

lawTitle: Play to People's Fantasies
scenarioText: A travel startup doesn't advertise cramped flights and long lines. Its ads sell escape: a solitary hammock, golden light, the version of yourself who finally slows down. Bookings soar even though the real trips are ordinary, because people are buying the dream, not the logistics.
explanationText: This is Play to People's Fantasies. The truth is often dull, but a vision of romance, escape, or transformation is irresistible. By selling the fantasy instead of the reality, the company draws people who will pay generously to believe.

lawTitle: Discover Each Man's Thumbscrew
scenarioText: A negotiator notices the opposing executive constantly mentions his kids and leaving early for their games. Instead of fighting on price, she offers a faster timeline that frees his evenings. Touched where it matters most, he concedes points he'd sworn he never would.
explanationText: This is Discover Each Man's Thumbscrew. Everyone has a single weakness or craving that, once found, unlocks them. By identifying what he truly valued and pressing gently there, she gained leverage that no amount of direct pressure could have produced.

lawTitle: Be Royal in Your Own Fashion: Act Like a King to Be Treated Like One
scenarioText: Two consultants have identical resumes. One apologizes for his rates, over-explains, and accepts every small request. The other quotes calmly, sets firm terms, and carries himself as if his time is precious. Clients treat the second as a premium expert and pay him double without blinking.
explanationText: This is Be Royal in Your Own Fashion: Act Like a King to Be Treated Like One. People take you at the value you place on yourself. By projecting quiet self-assurance and high standards, the second consultant made others accept his worth as obvious.

lawTitle: Master the Art of Timing
scenarioText: An investor watches a hyped stock climb while everyone urges him to buy in at the peak. He waits, patient through the frenzy, until the crowd panics and dumps it months later. Then he buys low. The move wasn't smarter than theirs; it was better timed.
explanationText: This is Master the Art of Timing. Acting is not enough; knowing precisely when to move and when to wait is what turns opportunity into advantage. By reading the rhythm of events and holding his patience, he let bad timing ruin the crowd and good timing reward him.

lawTitle: Disdain Things You Cannot Have: Ignoring Them Is the Best Revenge
scenarioText: After a breakup, one ex floods the other's socials with likes, subtweets, and 'accidental' run-ins, desperate to seem unbothered while obviously bothered. The other simply goes quiet, posts nothing, and lives well. The silence stings far more than any comeback could have.
explanationText: This is Disdain Things You Cannot Have: Ignoring Them Is the Best Revenge. Fighting for or fussing over something you've lost only advertises its power over you. Meeting it with genuine indifference denies it attention, and that dismissal is the sharpest response of all.

lawTitle: Create Compelling Spectacles
scenarioText: A phone company could just list specs, but instead it stages a darkened arena, a countdown, dramatic music, and a single glowing device rising on stage. People livestream it worldwide. The gadget is a modest upgrade, yet the theater around it makes everyone feel they witnessed history.
explanationText: This is Create Compelling Spectacles. Grand visuals and dramatic staging seize the imagination in ways that plain facts never can. By wrapping an ordinary product in unforgettable theater, the company commanded attention and made its power feel larger than life.

lawTitle: Think as You Like but Behave Like Others
scenarioText: A new hire privately thinks the company's beloved rituals are silly, but she joins the Friday chants, adopts the lingo, and never mocks the traditions out loud. Seen as a good fit, she's trusted and promoted, while a smarter colleague who openly scoffed gets frozen out.
explanationText: This is Think as You Like but Behave Like Others. Flaunting how differently you think only makes people feel judged and turns them against you. Keeping your unconventional views to yourself while outwardly blending in lets you belong, and gain influence, without drawing hostility.

lawTitle: Stir Up Waters to Catch Fish
scenarioText: In a heated contract dispute, one side stays icy calm while deliberately needling the other with small provocations and delays. The rival, enraged, blurts out threats and reveals his real deadline pressure. Now composed and informed, the calm side dictates the final terms.
explanationText: This is Stir Up Waters to Catch Fish. An angry, rattled opponent loses judgment and gives himself away, while you stay clear-headed. By provoking emotion in others while keeping your own in check, you turn their loss of control into your advantage.

lawTitle: Despise the Free Lunch
scenarioText: A small business jumps on a 'free' software platform to save money. Two years in, they're locked into its ecosystem, buried in surprise fees, and can't export their data without paying a fortune. A rival who paid upfront for solid tools now moves faster and cheaper.
explanationText: This is Despise the Free Lunch. What comes free usually carries a hidden cost, an obligation, or a trap. Paying full price for quality keeps you independent and often proves cheaper in the end, while the lure of something for nothing frequently ensnares you.

lawTitle: Avoid Stepping into a Great Man's Shoes
scenarioText: A wildly popular founder retires, and his hand-picked successor tries to run the company exactly as he did, echoing his catchphrases and mimicking his style. Every comparison makes him look smaller. The board grows restless, always measuring him against a shadow he can never match.
explanationText: This is Avoid Stepping into a Great Man's Shoes. Following directly in a legend's footsteps traps you in endless unflattering comparison. To establish real authority, a successor must carve out his own identity and territory rather than imitate the giant who came before.

lawTitle: Strike the Shepherd and the Sheep Will Scatter
scenarioText: A whole department is quietly rebelling, and management can't figure out why morale keeps collapsing. They finally realize one charismatic senior member is stirring every complaint. Once he's reassigned, the grumbling evaporates almost overnight and the team settles down, leaderless in their discontent.
explanationText: This is Strike the Shepherd and the Sheep Will Scatter. Trouble often flows from a single influential instigator, not the crowd that follows. Removing that one source of energy dissolves the whole problem, because the group had no independent momentum without its leader.

lawTitle: Work on the Hearts and Minds of Others
scenarioText: A new team lead could enforce her plan by pulling rank, but instead she learns each person's goals, ties the project to what they care about, and makes them feel it was partly their idea. They buy in willingly and work late without being asked, loyal rather than compliant.
explanationText: This is Work on the Hearts and Minds of Others. Coercion breeds quiet resistance, but winning people emotionally makes them cooperate on their own. By appealing to what they truly want, she secured genuine devotion instead of grudging obedience.

lawTitle: Disarm and Infuriate with the Mirror Effect
scenarioText: A rival keeps posting subtle digs at a small creator, expecting a meltdown. Instead she starts calmly echoing his exact tactics and phrasing back at him, reflecting his own behavior. Unsettled at seeing himself so clearly, he loses his cool publicly while she stays serene.
explanationText: This is Disarm and Infuriate with the Mirror Effect. Reflecting people's own actions back at them baffles and enrages them, because they can't attack what mimics themselves. By holding up a mirror instead of striking, she made his own conduct undo him.

lawTitle: Preach the Need for Change, but Never Reform Too Much at Once
scenarioText: A new principal, hired to modernize a tradition-bound school, announces on day one that she's scrapping the schedule, the grading system, and the beloved annual festival. Parents and teachers revolt. A colleague who instead changed one small thing a term, while praising tradition, transforms far more in the end.
explanationText: This is Preach the Need for Change, but Never Reform Too Much at Once. People accept the idea of change but panic at too much of it too fast, especially when familiar customs vanish. Wrapping gradual reform in respect for the past lets you transform things without triggering backlash.

lawTitle: Never Appear Too Perfect
scenarioText: A new colleague is flawless: top numbers, impeccable style, always right, never a hair out of place. Instead of admiration, he draws quiet envy and gossip, and people root for him to trip. A peer who openly laughs at her own small blunders is far better liked and protected.
explanationText: This is Never Appear Too Perfect. Seeming without flaw stirs envy, and envy works silently to bring you down. Admitting a harmless weakness now and then makes you human and approachable, defusing the resentment that perfection quietly breeds.

lawTitle: Do Not Go Past the Mark You Aimed For — In Victory, Learn When to Stop
scenarioText: A poker player has a fantastic night and hits his goal early, then, drunk on the streak, keeps playing to crush everyone. By dawn he's given back his winnings and more. He couldn't stop while he was ahead, and momentum turned into ruin.
explanationText: This is Do Not Go Past the Mark You Aimed For — In Victory, Learn When to Stop. The rush of success tempts you to press on past your original goal, where luck runs out and mistakes multiply. Knowing when to halt and lock in your win is as vital as the victory itself.

lawTitle: Assume Formlessness
scenarioText: A nimble startup refuses to lock itself into one rigid business model. When a giant competitor copies its main product, it has already pivoted to a new niche, and by the time the giant adapts, it has shifted again. Impossible to pin down, it survives rivals ten times its size.
explanationText: This is Assume Formlessness. A fixed shape is a target that larger, stronger opponents can study and crush. By staying fluid, adaptable, and willing to change form, the startup made itself impossible to corner and outlasted forces that should have overwhelmed it.
